---
title: "Research"
description: "Research statement: multimodal foundation models for tabular data"
date-modified: last-modified
---

## Statement

The bottleneck of tabular machine learning is not the model — it is the
*schema*. Foundation models for text, images, audio, and video have all
benefited from the assumption that the input space is universal: a token is
a token, a pixel is a pixel. Tables break this assumption. Each table has
its own column names, its own units, its own ontology, and increasingly its
own multimodal cells (images of products, free-text reviews, geocoded
locations, embedded JSON).

My research is on **multimodal tabular foundation models that pretrain
across heterogeneous schemas** and condition downstream prediction on
column-aware representations. Three threads:

### 1. Cross-schema pretraining

How do we pretrain on millions of tables when no two share the same
columns? Recent work — TabPFN, TabuLa-8B, CARTE, GTL — has shown that
column-aware tokenization and masked-cell objectives can transfer
across schemas. The open question is *how far this scales* and where
the inductive biases break.

### 2. Multimodal columns

A column can be a number, a category, a free-text comment, an image, or a
nested record. Most tabular pretraining ignores everything beyond the first
two. I am working on encoders that treat each column type with its own
modality-appropriate embedder while sharing a fused decoder for downstream
prediction.

### 3. Probabilistic structure for tabular FM

Tabular tasks are often risk-sensitive (medicine, finance, materials). The
probabilistic-DL community (where my co-supervisor van de Meent built
[ProbTorch](https://github.com/probtorch/probtorch)) has tools — amortized
inference, latent-variable factorizations, Bayesian post-training — that
have not yet landed in tabular FMs. I want to bring them.

## Path

I co-led the [ML4H 2025 paper](https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.25759) on a
*generalization gap* between correlated and non-correlated multiple-instance
learning architectures, using a Bayes-optimal synthetic benchmark with the
Hughes Lab at Tufts. Earlier work in materials science (with the CANELa Lab
at Pittsburgh) gave me the engineering chops to actually train models at
non-trivial scale: a weight-initialization method delivering 71% RMSE
reduction on nanoparticle stability prediction
[(Acc. Chem. Res. 2023)](https://doi.org/10.1021/acs.accounts.2c00646).

Production ML at KEF Robotics, MGH/Harvard, and ContentsPal taught me
something different: that the most important property of a research model is
whether it survives contact with real data and real users. I will keep this
discipline through the PhD.

## What I am looking for

Collaboration on:

- Tabular pretraining benchmarks beyond TabPFN-bench
- Column-type extension: image cells, free text, structured nested records
- Bayesian post-training of tabular FMs
- Open releases of trained checkpoints, evaluation harnesses, and reproducible code

If any of this overlaps with what you are building, please reach out at
[dennis.loevlie@tufts.edu](mailto:dennis.loevlie@tufts.edu).
